You alert on breaches, ideally before consumers notice, and track an error budget. The goal is to detect issues proactively through monitoring rather than learning of them from a stakeholder's complaint.

questions.qText: What are the five pillars of data observability?
questions.header: Stage 8 - Data Quality, Governance & Reliability Interview Q&A

blocks.text: The five pillars are freshness (is the data up to date), volume (is the amount of data within expected ranges — completeness of rows), schema (have the structure, types, or columns changed unexpectedly), distribution (are the values within acceptable ranges and statistical norms — detecting anomalies and nulls), and lineage (the upstream/downstream map that lets you trace where an issue originated and what it impacts).

Together they extend application observability concepts to data, enabling automated detection of data downtime — periods where data is missing, wrong, or stale — before it corrupts downstream decisions.

questions.qText: How do you handle PII and comply with GDPR in a data pipeline?
questions.header: Stage 8 - Data Quality, Governance & Reliability Interview Q&A

blocks.text: First, identify and classify PII (names, emails, identifiers, location) and minimize collection. Protect it with masking, tokenization, or encryption — often applying it as early as possible (even pre-load in an ETL step) so raw PII does not spread. Apply access controls so only authorized roles and queries can see sensitive columns, using column- and row-level security.

GDPR also requires honoring the right to be forgotten (delete an individual's data on request), which is why ACID table formats with DELETE/MERGE matter — append-only lakes make targeted deletion hard. You also need data retention limits, consent tracking, purpose limitation, and an auditable record (lineage) of where personal data flows. Pseudonymization plus a separately secured key mapping is a common technique to retain analytical utility while protecting identity.

questions.qText: What is the difference between masking, tokenization, and encryption?
questions.header: Stage 8 - Data Quality, Governance & Reliability Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Masking obscures data by replacing or partially hiding it (showing only the last four digits of a card), often irreversibly for display purposes — good for non-production environments and dashboards where the real value is not needed. Tokenization replaces a sensitive value with a non-sensitive surrogate token, with the real value stored in a secure token vault, so the mapping is reversible only by authorized systems and the token can preserve format for analytics joins.

Encryption transforms data with a key so it is unreadable without decryption; it protects data at rest and in transit and is reversible with the key. They are complementary: encrypt for storage/transport security, tokenize to keep PII out of analytical stores while retaining referential ability, and mask for display and lower environments.

questions.qText: How do you choose a partitioning strategy for scale and performance?
questions.header: Stage 8 - Data Quality, Governance & Reliability Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Partition on the column most queries filter on — usually a date (event_date) — so the engine prunes irrelevant partitions and scans far less data. Pick a granularity that yields reasonably sized partitions: too coarse (one giant partition) gives no pruning, too fine (high-cardinality keys like user_id, or hourly when daily suffices) creates the small-files problem and metadata overhead.

Avoid partitioning on high-cardinality columns; use clustering/sorting within partitions for secondary filter columns instead. Target partition file sizes around 128MB-1GB, align partitioning with how data arrives so loads can overwrite cleanly (supporting idempotency), and revisit the strategy as query patterns and data volume evolve. Iceberg's hidden partitioning lets you change this later without rewriting all data.

questions.qText: What is a data contract and how does it improve reliability?
questions.header: Stage 8 - Data Quality, Governance & Reliability Interview Q&A

blocks.text: A data contract is an explicit, enforced agreement between a data producer and its consumers defining the schema, semantics, quality guarantees, and SLAs of a dataset or event — essentially an API contract for data. It shifts ownership of quality upstream to the producer rather than leaving consumers to defensively clean whatever arrives.

It improves reliability by catching breaking changes at the source: a producer's CI validates that a schema change is compatible (much like Schema Registry compatibility modes for Kafka) before it ships, preventing the classic failure where an upstream rename silently breaks downstream pipelines. Contracts make expectations explicit, testable, and versioned.

questions.qText: How do you safely recover from a pipeline failure and run a backfill?
questions.header: Stage 8 - Data Quality, Governance & Reliability Interview Q&A

blocks.text: First contain: stop downstream propagation and assess the blast radius using lineage to see which tables and dashboards are affected. Identify the failed date range or partitions. Because tasks are idempotent and partitioned, you re-run only the affected logical dates so the reprocessing overwrites the bad partitions rather than duplicating data.

Use the raw/bronze layer (preserved source data) or time travel to recover the correct inputs, run the backfill (e.g. an Airflow backfill over the date range), then validate with the same quality checks before re-publishing. Finally, do a post-mortem and add a check or alert so the same failure is caught automatically next time. The whole approach depends on idempotency, partitioning by date, and retained raw data.

questions.qText: What is the difference between fail-fast and quarantine strategies for bad data?
questions.header: Stage 8 - Data Quality, Governance & Reliability Interview Q&A

blocks.text: Fail-fast halts the pipeline as soon as a critical quality check fails, so no bad data reaches consumers. It is the right choice for business-critical correctness where serving wrong data is worse than serving none — but it can block the entire load over a few bad records and demands prompt operator attention.

Quarantine (dead-letter) routes the offending records to a separate location for later inspection while letting the valid majority flow through, maximizing availability and isolating problems without stopping everything. The choice depends on criticality: fail-fast for strict correctness and small/critical datasets, quarantine for high-volume streams where partial progress is acceptable and you can reprocess the quarantined records once fixed.

questions.qText: How do you detect data anomalies in volume and distribution?
questions.header: Stage 8 - Data Quality, Governance & Reliability Interview Q&A

blocks.text: For volume, track row counts (and bytes) per load and compare against expectations — static thresholds, day-over-day or week-over-week deltas, or a rolling baseline with standard-deviation bands. A sudden drop often means an upstream feed broke or a join filtered too aggressively; a spike can mean duplication or a backfill. For distribution, monitor per-column statistics over time: null rate, distinct count, min/max/mean, and category frequencies.

Alert when a metric drifts beyond its expected band — for instance the null rate of a key column jumps, or a previously rare category suddenly dominates (data drift). Tools like Great Expectations encode these as expectations, and observability platforms can learn baselines automatically. The aim is to catch silent data corruption that schema checks alone would miss.
